g.data-narration: Similarity-based learning predicts by comparing new data to stored examples, unlike model-based approaches that build explicit functions.
Model-based learning builds explicit models like decision trees while instance-based learning stores data and defers computation to prediction time.
The core principle is birds of a feather flock together: similar data points in feature space likely have similar outputs or class labels.
g.text: Core Principle: "Birds of a feather flock together"
Similar data points in feature space likely have similar outputs (class labels or target values).

g.data-narration: K-Nearest Neighbors is the most famous similarity-based algorithm, used for both classification (categorical outputs) and regression (continuous outputs).
g.text: Most famous similarity-based algorithm. Used for both:

g.data-narration: K-NN works in five steps: store all training data, receive a query point, compute distances to all points, find K nearest neighbors, and aggregate their outputs.
In classification, K-NN finds the K nearest neighbors and takes a majority vote. With K=5 and 3 Class A vs 2 Class B, it predicts Class A.
For regression tasks, K-NN outputs the mean or median of target values from K nearest neighbors instead of majority voting.
g.text: Instead of majority vote, output is the mean (or median) of target values of K neighbors.

g.data-narration: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points, calculated as the square root of sum of squared differences.
g.text: Straight-line distance between two points. Most common for continuous features.

g.data-narration: Other distance metrics include Manhattan for grid-like paths, Hamming for categorical data, and Cosine similarity for text and sparse data.
g.text: Manhattan: grid-like paths | Hamming: position differences | Cosine: angle between vectors

g.data-narration: K-NN is a lazy learner - it stores data during training but does zero computation until a prediction is needed.
g.text: Lazy Learner: No Explicit Training Phase
K-NN does no work during training - it simply stores the data. All computation happens at prediction time.

g.data-narration: The curse of dimensionality causes all points to become nearly equidistant in high dimensions, making distance-based methods unreliable.
g.text: As features (dimensions) increase, distance becomes less meaningful. All points become nearly equidistant!
Solution: Feature selection or dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA) before applying K-NN.

g.data-narration: Feature scaling is essential because features with larger ranges would dominate distance calculations, skewing predictions.
g.text: Features with larger ranges dominate distance calculations. Standardization or Min-Max scaling is mandatory.

g.data-narration: Choosing K involves a bias-variance tradeoff: small K leads to complex boundaries (overfitting), large K leads to smooth boundaries (underfitting).
g.text: K is a hyperparameter controlling complexity. Choose through cross-validation.

g.data-narration: K-NN advantages include simplicity, natural handling of multi-class problems, no distributional assumptions, and easy adaptation to new data.
K-NN disadvantages include slow prediction, memory intensity, sensitivity to irrelevant features, and vulnerability to the curse of dimensionality.
Key takeaways: K-NN predicts by finding similar stored instances, uses distance metrics and requires feature scaling, and is a lazy learner suitable for recommendations and anomaly detection.

g.text: Core: Predict based on similarity to stored instances
Critical: Distance metric, K value, Feature scaling
Type: Lazy learner (computation at query time)
Challenge: Curse of dimensionality, large datasets

g.data-narration: Real-world applications of similarity-based learning include recommendation systems, medical diagnosis by comparing patient records, and anomaly detection in security systems.

g.text: "Users who liked X also liked Y"
Compare patient to similar historical cases
Flag outliers far from normal patterns
